So, you may be known as somebody who's very difficult to work with, and you're known for your ability to take dental practices and magically ten times them in size overnight. You may be known as an enormously compassionate, empathetic father figure. You would be known for some competency. You have to pick what those things are and build your shortlist and then make sure that you do everything strategically to reinforce the shortlist that you've decided on. So for a number of years I did work for a guy by the name of...he doesn't do much anymore. His name is Dick Sutfin, S-U-T-P-A-G-N. And Dick's were studying for a variety of reasons. One is he was a metaphysical community star for a number of years. And a past lives person, big seminar once a year in Sedona where 500 people come and they all lay on the floor and roll around and flop and vomit and stuff and have simultaneous past lives experiences and return to Atlantis and they were all Napoleon. Nobody by the way in their past life was ever like a beggar. They were all emperors and emperors and knights of the round table and queen of Atlantis and so forth. But anyway, so Dick had a bestselling book by the way on the New York Times list for a number of years called Past Lives, Future Loves, all about past lives stuff. Which our joke was that the title should have been Past Lives, Future Alimony based upon the number of times that Dick was married. But anyway, so this is Dick's positioning. So Dick for example had to have long hair. Anything else would have been unacceptable for who he was. And he couldn't come in the room in a three-piece pinstripe suit and a tie that would not be acceptable for who he was. Now who Dick actually was was an advertising copywriter and executive from New York who got tired of being and had three-piece pinstripe suits and ties and that's pretty much all he ever wore while he was in New York and short hair. But he got tired of being an advertising copywriter and went in search of a niche and a market and so forth. And he transformed himself into this larger than life character. But he had to make sure everything was congruent with the short list of things that he decided that he was going to be known as and known for. Hence the Future Alimony problem because they kept marrying him thinking they were getting that thing they saw up on stage. And you know who set up on the roof in the rain and wrote poetry. And then after about three months of discovering that he was us. Enormous disillusionment set in and then off we go to divorce court. So you choose this thing and then you build it. Now if you don't consciously choose and you don't consciously and strategically build it by the way you wind up with a reputation anyway. It's just that it gets written for you by others. And once it gets in place with a constituency with a market by the way it's very hard to change. So you really want to give a lot of thought to what your short list ought to be. If we were taking the time to do workshop stuff today I would put you all around tables and for the next 15 minutes we'd sit there and write our short list and then we'd all compare our short list and stuff for the sake of time. I've chosen not to do that. But you ought to go home and do it. You ought to help get somebody to help you help each other do it. You ought to give some very serious thought to it. And then what you will find is there are ways that you get to strategically use the reputation you've designed. And so by the way part of designing it is deciding how you're going to strategically use it. Give you a couple of examples which are on your next page. So I have very carefully cultivated a reputation of not being a warm and fuzzy fellow. It is very deliberate on my part. That doesn't mean I'm hiding a warm and fuzzy fellow deep inside. That's not at all what I am telling you. But I have very carefully cultivated the reputation of not being this. There's a number of reasons for it. One, there are certain kinds of things that people are prone not to discuss with me, not to tell me because of that reputation. It's things I don't particularly like talking about or dealing with but without that reputation they would be telling me about that. The evidence is when Carl and I were divorced shortly after the divorce people started to talk to me about things they never talked to me about before. That changed the reputation and gave them permission to now talk to me about their marital problems. Well I don't want to talk about their marital problems. I claim zero expertise in this category. It's actually a name to have the conversation. I am not the person to go get this advice from. I don't know who that person is but I'm not it. But it sparked an enormous amount of these conversations that had never happened before and it's gradually all stopped and it doesn't happen now. Well I did that like on purpose. I didn't want to have those kind of conversations. By not having the warm and fuzzy reputation I get currency out of rather ordinary and mundane acts that otherwise you wouldn't. I'll just say very quickly I'm not suggesting that you'd rather hear it adopt my shortlist. I am suggesting you've got to decide on a shortlist for yourself but I'm using some of my choices and decisions as an example. There has to be content match too. It would be harder to take this position if I was doing a lot of personal growth stuff rather than what I do for a living. So for example because I'm not a warm and fuzzy person and have a reputation of not being a warm and fuzzy person and being all business and all bottom line and all hard nosed and all tough and all that. A simple handwritten note from me acknowledging that their kid got accepted into college or their dog passed away or their whatever has far more impact than the same note from someone with a reputation of being a warm, fuzzy, involved in personal lives person. So the strategic use of my reputation lets me take ordinary and mundane acts and give them enormous significance in the relationship between me and my client. So I have a client for example. It's a client off and on over the years who at times has given me a great deal of money and then periods of time have gone by where they have given me no money. This client was diagnosed last year with throat cancer, some kind of throat cancer. Our relationship is not frequent enough that I even knew. So I heard several months after the fact through a third party. So at Christmas, we sent Christmas cards, I jotted a handwritten note and it took the time to write a couple paragraphs and sent it to them. Now I didn't do it to be manipulative or strategic at this particular point in time. As I say, I don't need to do that anymore actually and I don't really want any more business from them and I got all the business. That was the furthest thing from my mind. However, because it is so out of character with my reputation and with that relationship, I got the long, winded, five page emotional letter back but then like two weeks later, their company is calling and suddenly trying to give me large sums of money to come and do things which hasn't happened in almost three years. Now any number of people they deal with who have a different reputation could send him exactly the same note and not have the same significance and not have the same result. So there are strategic uses for the things that you have in your reputation because I very carefully limit endorsements and I'm kind of stingy with them and kind of picky about what I endorse. I get a lot of currency for example out of agreeing to write a blurb for somebody's book. Somebody who gives out blurbs for book covers like giving out M&M's on Halloween who shall not be mentioned in this room but there are famous people who have a blurb on every book that was published last year and the book could be pure crap and they've written a blurb for it. Not only don't they understand, they're getting some free promotion yes but they're actually damaging their reputation with the general public but beyond that they have no power now out of giving the blurb to certain people because everybody knows they give blurbs to everybody so getting one from them is no big thing. I don't feel any sense of obligation when I get a blurb from that guy. I don't feel any need to be reciprocal when I get a blurb from that guy. It's like so what? But there are certain people you get a blurb from them you feel like oh man I really owe the guy. I really what can I do for you? So your reputation as you build it and reinforce it gives you currency, gives you the ability to have impact or not have impact with certain things that you do. For many years Bill is much more generous than I about this. For many years you old timers will know at my events there weren't very many amenities. Like I wasn't big on feeding people you know. Now Bill is much more generous about this but also those of you that put on meetings know hotel contracts have changed over the years and so it's relatively hard to get meeting space without committing to a certain amount of food service. So he's more generous than I but some of the generosity is forcibly imposed. I didn't have to but because I never did every so often I would for example spring the okay I decided to buy lunch for everybody today and we're all going to stay in the room and I'm going to do an extra Q&A session on everybody and I typically would do that immediately before a session where I or somebody else was going to ask everybody for money. Well buying lunch for them if you always buy lunch for them and you're expected to buy lunch for them well it doesn't get you anything. Buying lunch for them when you never buy lunch for them that gets you a lot. It's the same lunch. The only difference is the reputation of the person buying the lunch right that's the only difference. So there's strategic uses for everything you put into your reputation there's also the issue of forgiveness or condemnation so just as the same act for two different people can have two profoundly different kinds of significance depending upon their reputations. The same act by two different people can be easily forgiven or jumped upon and turned into a big evil thing totally depending upon the reputation of the person. The person of growth and religious leaders share the worst position in this in terms of bad behavior in general because they lecture to everybody about good behavior all the time then when they engage in bad behavior their light burned at the stake for it. If you don't lecture about good behavior to people and in fact kind of have a reputation for some bad behavior then the exact same act of bad behavior is water off a duck's back your constituency pretty much ignores it. So depending upon your reputation certain acts of behavior will have one kind of impact or another kind of impact. It's real important not to commit yourself to a reputation that is so in conflict with behavior of yours that is likely to be observed, seen, known about or become public that you are going to be severely condemned for it. A little checklist for you on the next page. You build one of these things that number one you can live with and live in for a distance. Simple example. Bad idea to have done this thing if I wasn't planning on keeping it because I can't make it go away now until I quit. Too many photographs, too many videos, too many promotional pieces, too many bobbleheads, too many, it literally is part of the reputation. I'm known for it so if I didn't really like it growing the thing wasn't such a smart idea. Same thing with bigger issues. You got to build one that you can live with for a long period of time and it gives you a little bit of wiggle room and that does not set impossible standards that you can't stick to. So for example, if you have a reputation for, boy he's the guy who calls everybody back within 30 minutes. That might serve you well early. And you sustain it over a period of time. Not if you're successful because eventually there's more people calling you than you can call back. So now what happens? Now everybody's pissed off and aggravated. So you got to be able to live with it and it can't set up impossible standards. You should develop one that facilitates influence, power and control. So for example, a reputation of somebody who is very relaxed, casual, tolerant, whatever happens is not particularly useful in running coaching groups because you will eventually have trouble keeping the children from hitting each other with the blocks. A reputation of someone who can occasionally fly off the handle and visibly be seen doing it, that's actually a little more useful because now there's a little bit of, we really don't want to set him off going on. That will be magnetic to your constituency of choice. Gene Simmons has a reputation. George Will has a reputation. Neither would be particularly magnetic to the other's constituency. So you have to think about who is your market? What kind of person do they admire? Do they envy? Do they want to be around? Do they wish they were? Preferably something that has evergreen appeal. Not something that's going to be here today, gone tomorrow, that's really trendy, that's really short term. This is pretty important. Something that at its sum total is easy for people to grasp and remember. They need to be able to give an elevator speech for you. They need to be able to explain who you are and what you are to themselves and to others, like short, not long. And that it's difficult to undermine or attack. Ralph Nader has a populist, pretty consistent. He's kept it consistent over the years. It's difficult to undermine and attack. When he campaigns, he still stays in people's houses instead of hotels to save money. John Edwards has decided to run as a populist. Lots of luck. Like he just built a 22,000 square foot house and clear cut the entire property in order to do it. It's going to be very hard for John Edwards to be the champion of the little minimum wage worker guy. That's not going to be easy to sustain. And it's going to be very, very easy to go at. All you got to do is show the pictures of the 22,000 square foot house. Very easy to attack. So you got to take care about this. You got to give a lot of conscious thought to it. You got to think it through. You got to arrive at decisions about what it's going to be and how you're going to use it. And then you got to strive for constant congruency in a lot of different categories. What do you want? A break? Oh, I know. I'm getting there. I'm getting there. I'm getting there. I was just trying to find a logical place. And what's your problem? You could go in and out the door anytime you want to. Yeah? I mean, gee whiz. I mean, I didn't see them. You can tell by the looks on their face when you've pressed them a little too far. So this is a real important piece of your puzzle. And it shouldn't be accident. It shouldn't be happenstance. It shouldn't be random. Should be very, very carefully thought through. Purposeful, strategic. You need the short list as a reference point to make all of your other decisions. What am I going to say? What am I going to write? Who am I going to hang out with? Where am I going to go? What am I going to wear? What charity am I going to support? On and on and on and on and on and on and on and on and on. When people ask you what books you're reading, your answer needs to be congruent with your reputation. It doesn't matter what you're reading, by the way. But the answer has got to be congruent with your reputation. If you rattle off a bunch of books that are completely incongruent with your reputation that you're trying to sustain, you mess people up. And you undermine what it is that you're trying to do. So this picture you build over here, this short list you build over here, has to be a constantly used reference tool to govern every other decision you make about your business. I guess you guys do need a break. Let's try and do it quickly, please. I'm going to tell you something. If I paid what you paid to be in this room, I'd be running to get back in. I wouldn't be strolling, baby. I'd be running. This goes to show you can't take enough money from them. You know what I'm saying? That's what you can do. The behavior is the same as if you were at a $300 seminar. All right? I mean, I'd be hustling. This is my favorite disdain for the marketplace cartoon. For those of you that can't read it, there's one dinosaur talking all the rest of them. The picture is pretty bleak. The world's climates are changing. The mammals are taking over. And we all have a brain about the size of a walnut. I keep that around to remind me what I'm dealing with. Begin by being known for something. You want to settle on one great competency, one great characteristic, one great story, one great thing about you that you are going to leverage everything else off of. So like in your case, it was make a whole lot more money in the mortgage business, work less, deal with a certain type of customer. And then you start to fuzzy it, and you start to fuzzy it, and you start to fuzzy it. But you need to pick something. Almost everybody goes broad to narrow, narrow to broad, broad to narrow. It may be easier to look at this in your notebook than it is to look up on a screen. My old joke itself is explanatory, so we'll just move on. But so a lot of people go broad to narrow, back out to broad. They start narrow, they go narrow to broad. Actually during the length of your career, you'll be moving from one to the other and back again and in and out. This is just sort of a quick little chart of me. And so at the very beginning, very broad. I mean I started back with, hey, here's direct marketing for any business and any sales guy. It doesn't matter what you do, it doesn't matter what business you're in, it doesn't matter what the size is, I'm going to give you better marketing. Then it got a little narrower to magnetic marketing, but mostly what I was known for, for almost a decade, if you acquist people about it, it would be the three step mail campaign and then it would be long copy. Well those are both things pretty narrow. Then get broad again and get even broader, fuzzy the edges, start to be talking about entrepreneurial strategies, success strategies and so forth. Now from there back narrow again, starting to develop the marketing to the affluent category which is at least as narrow as magnetic marketing was, I've been market niched, I've been non-market niched, gone from narrow to broad, from broad to narrow, you will do the same thing. If you look at, everybody's known for one thing and then they fuzzy the edges. So if you look at our friend Trump for example, he actually started pretty much being viewed as a negotiation guy under real estate. Now he's fuzzy the edges to wealth success strategies in general. Now he's fuzzy the edges to lifestyle issues in general. Dale Carnegie began purely about how to stand up and give the speech without throwing out. That was Dale Carnegie's position in the marketplace. So all their corporate clients, as soon as somebody got to the place in their corporate career that they had to be able to do a group presentation, they sent him to Dale Carnegie. And Dale Carnegie leveraged it, got fuzzier and broader, got into sales, got into leadership. Dr. Phil is kind of funny I think actually, but I still find him doing weight loss I'm using. But a quick example of can anybody do it. Here's a, I just pulled this out of DM news, here's a guy that is an executive recruiter in the direct marketing industry. So meaning companies hire him to go find me a VP of this, thus or so, right? Now he's started a new coaching business for direct marketers seeking a job. So that's got to take money from the other side. Now of course he doesn't know any better, so let's see here. They spend hours taking an individual through a proven multi-step process, DBDB, and I'm getting 175 bucks an hour, so ain't that terrific. By the way, he just told me again, who is it? The, the... International Coaches Federation. Okay. Which I assume is like some kind of NSA for coaches. Is that, is that fair? They just hired... Price Waterhouse to do a big income survey. Average annual income of everybody in their Federation is 50,000 bucks. Right? So... Yeah, they sent it out. Well remember NSA did the same thing some years ago. NSA did the same thing. They sent out, yeah, like they're bragging about this. Right? So you know, two of your clients exceeded their average... Okay. Okay. Let's jump to... This is pretty important, so I can't skip it. Let's go to page 13. The keywords are at the bottom. A lot of your work is done for you before you arrive on a scene or utter a single word because your reputation precedes you. Now this is really important. One way or another, the heavy lifting should be done before you quote, arrive on a scene. So before they come to you, before you get to them, somehow the heavy lifting ought to be done. The worst thing. I've done it three times in my entire life. Twice early and once later but under duress and I was an idiot to do it, is going into a meeting of a bunch of people from a company who don't know you from Adam and you are now there to pitch. Not a place you want to be. Not a place you want to be. So one way or another, you've got to have your reputation precede you. That may be the big box of stuff they have to read and listen to. The big thing they've got to fill out. It may be the seminar they have to sit through before they're allowed to by coaching. The CDs they have to listen to before they can have a conversation with you. The word of mouth what other people have said about you, the champion in the company, your book but you've got to construct a process that has your reputation precede you so all the heavy lifting is done by the time you show up. We'll talk more about the tale but it's important to at least get it conceptually. And so let me just jump to 16 because I'll give you an example of now a specific strategy. So this latest consulting gig which Bill and I are doing jointly, its source was a person of influence in the company. So there's a Kennedy fan who begins the process of trying to convince his company that they should come to us and then he gets another guy and they get another guy and then they go up the chain. At some point now somebody from the company contacts my office. Now here's where are we going to have the reputation precede or are we going to do the heavy lifting right? So what most people do, so let's take Dan Cricksey saying where are you? Okay so last night at the IBA meeting he's because he's starting to be known. He now has somebody calling him who wants him to privately coach them and she's a fairly apparently successful and substantial coach in her field and now the very worst thing in the world he can do is talk to her. That's premature because it gives no opportunity for the heavy lifting to be done before he talks to her. The very best thing is he needs an intake procedure with some steps in it so that by the time they do talk to you that's like an accomplishment. And the very process has set up greater emotional commitment, greater desire to get you now that it's been so hard to get you. So we for years have had this sort of intake procedure at my office where they are told go prepare no less than one page, no more than two pages explaining why it is you want to talk to him and what it is you need and what it is you want to do and facts it in here. And then I'll forward it to him and then he'll decide whether or not he's going to talk to you and if he is going to talk to you then I'll be back to you and we'll set up a phone appointment. Well now some people of course will fear the person they're going to lose because they won't go through those steps but you're better off moving on to the next person if in fact you have enough deal flow than you are trying to do the heavy lifting yourself. So now what happens first of all is the very fact that they now go back and they go through those steps which are weird to them. So from the very beginning I'm changing the way they normally behave and the way they normally get things done because normally if they're thinking about buying something from somebody that person jumps on them and tries to sell it to them. I don't want to do that. I want to essentially do the opposite. So they've expressed an interest in buying something from me now I want to not want to sell it to them. I wanted to have to fight every step of the way to get the opportunity to be able to buy it. I want to put stuff in their hands so now okay we do go back to them and they do get a phone appointment but prior to the phone appointment now the big box of stuff gets FedEx to them. With books and CDs and newsletters and relevant testimonial letters and when we go through sample documents I'm going to show you some of the correspondence from this particular situation so you get even a better feel for it but so a relevant package of stuff some of it the same you would send everybody some of it not the same you would send everybody arrives into their hands with instructions about what to pay attention to first what to pay attention to second what to look at before we get on the phone. So I have put my reputation in a box and I have put it in their hands to do as much of the work as possible before I have the phone call. So I don't on the phone call have to have the conversation of tell us the 12 reasons why we should hire you convince us to hire you see I don't want to have that phone conversation the only phone conversation I want to have is them convincing me I should let them or just the detail conversation of when where and how much those are only two conversations I want to have so they get stuff then in this case we made them double opt in so they got to come back and request the phone appointment a second time and then finally they get the phone appointment well by the time they're at the phone appointment they are now a little frustrated they're a little antsy and anxious and they are pretty much determined they are going to get this guy if it's an all possible to get him because already deeply emotionally committed to doing so as opposed to me making it easy for him and then having to sell him hard now in this particular case they then needed buy-in from a whole group of people because this is a corporate client so we had to go through this whole set of steps again now they're going to put me in front of a group of people from their company who have to buy into this project well I don't just go from talking to them to stand up in front of the group and pitch and I want to do that I go from phone interview to full day with them to planning the day with the group even writing in the CEO's voice the letter that is being sent out to everybody in the group who is coming to the meeting pre-framing them for why they are at the meeting and what's going to happen and who Kennedy is I get a book in all their hands with instructions that they should read it before the meeting yes some will some won't but better some than none we get them on a list and fax them some information before the meeting I exercise a lot of control over what happens I just don't walk in show up and do a pitch meeting I don't want it to be that I want the meeting to be framed as an explanation and question and answer session to determine whether or not it's appropriate for me to accept this project from them and work with them as a group I don't want it framed about them deciding whether or not it's appropriate it's about me deciding whether it's appropriate coming for the candy or you coming to talk okay just a second this hey glazes bill bill whoa break see I would have got there I had a break plan no no it's not another break so you want the work done if you turn to 14 what's most important here is you want the work done to the greatest degree possible before you arrive right so that they are if you view them on a continuum of one to ten this way and one is don't know guy from Adam think he may have answered to my problem might want to use him to ten absolutely determined to get him if it is all possible to get him we desperately need him and only need him you damn sure want to pass the five and by the way it's not just about getting the sale it's about everything that's going to happen after you get the sale because if you got the sale by selling keeping control of them after the sale is going to be very difficult if you got the sale by them selling you then keeping control of them after the fact is going to be a lot easier so this is not just about getting somebody into a coaching program this is not just about getting somebody into a consulting contract this is about what will color the relationship forever how it starts has a lot to do with how it's going to go and how it's going to end and the worst thing you can do is be an impatient instinctive sales person which we all are and want to jump on it too quick and sell it too hard especially if you're good at it so you know I can get a check out of a rock in a pay toilet so the my instinctive reaction is as soon as somebody who might have a check in their pocket rears their head I mean I know how to knock them down and get to check in five minutes but that doesn't mean that the relationship is going to be good after I get it so I have to restrain my impulses and implement a process that gives me control over the client not just the check from the client too many people care just about making the sale not about what's going to happen after the sale you can make you can sell them by brute force you can put them in coaching by brute force you can get a consulting client by brute force you can't keep them around for ten years if you've got them by brute force that's what's important to know yes I'm wondering what you did in the meeting with the CEO that convinced him that you needed to write a letter in his voice and do this whole process to bring you back for a paid meeting with the group I well the short answer is I told him and so let me embellish the short answer first and then I'll give you a blow by bowl long answer the short answer is important because my positioning from word go is there there to be told what to do and I tell them what to do and they do it so it's not like gee here's here's something that would be good to do will you do it it's here's what we're going to do right and it would be better if I wrote it than you write it because I'm better at that than you are so I'll write it if there's something in it you don't like we can talk about it so all of it is about I'm gonna tell you what to do and the real important thing to keep in mind about all that is for the most part again that's really what everybody wants they want certainty they want somebody to tell them what to do and it doesn't much matter whether they're making ten million dollars a year or ten cents a year doesn't much make any difference they're looking for somebody to tell them what to do the longer answer to your question is they're the ones who needed the group meeting so we had a full day so my normal procedure is the intake procedure I talked about ultimately every new relationship begins with a paid day of consulting and then at the end of the day maybe something happens maybe it doesn't right so they pay for the day of consulting and in this case they paid a premium because they had they wanted to be wedged in at a time I didn't want to wedge them in and I forced them to come to where I was going to be on vacation anyway except so I inconvenienced them and all of that okay I did it a because I was going to be I only did it's all I had and B because inconveniencing them is a good thing to do like the worst thing to do is go to their office that's like the worst thing to do okay so so now we have the day so my normal reaction now at the end of the day is okay we get it will you do this for us and how much does it cost for you to do this right so I get that but then I get revealed to me that really even though I'm in the room with the president he doesn't have the authority to actually do anything without buying from this group of franchise owners well quite frankly I'm immediately pissed off okay because I didn't know that that is like a new piece of information and I was like planning on a contract so I'm now like hot because I wedged them in I spent a day with them and actually they can't this guy can't give me money so and I do my best work when I'm hot I'm serious my sales from in front of the room are always best but somebody really pisses me off right before I go do it so I mean I'm like aggravated you know and I let it be known I'm aggravated you know I mean so I asked whether or not he he and his two cohorts that are with him came with their private parts or without their private parts and I ask it in a way not as nice way as I just said it and they explain it's really not about that it's corporate structure you know we if we don't get these guys to buy in there's no point and etc etc so you know I say okay I'll you know so what has to happen well we got to put them on a room and you got to tell them what you just told us right so I say I ain't happy about it and I'll think about it yeah so now they leave and now they start bugging us you know to do this so then I agree to do it for another fee so they got to pay the day fee I ain't coming doing a pitch meeting for free and here's what has to happen before we do the pitch meeting you're going to do this you're going to do this and you're going to do this and they do this and they do this and they do this now by the way they ain't capable of doing much so the other reason specifically to write the letter for him is because he's not capable of writing the letter that's going to have the effect that I wanted to have and by the time I explained to him what I wanted him to do you know we'd all be old and gray okay and and he doesn't even realize what it was he just sent it okay so it is really about just telling them what to do okay section two you are now on page 17 what we sell as I said what we don't sell that everybody thinks we sell is the top list we don't actually sell credentials because make no mistake they don't care about credentials they don't even know what credentials mean half of this alphabet soup stuff people put after their name nobody even knows what the devil it means except the people who are issuing the alphabet soup and the peers who are concerned about the alphabet soup nobody checks credentials nobody makes any decisions based on credentials so patient Charlie so Charlie's average case size at his dental practice is like 40,000 bucks right a lot of cases are 7080 $90,000 right how many patients do you know of in the past I don't know three years who've actually gone and called the dental the American dental academy or you know somehow checked out your credentials to do what it is that you do anybody verbalize it to you yeah okay so that's the big deal they check them out on the internet well how you check about the internet you know what's people do they go look at your own website that's how they check you out on the internet okay nobody checks no check so how important is that to them it's not they're making that this they're making that decision totally based on other criteria now the same thing to do of course if you're about to spend say $80,000 on dentistry would be to meticulously go get a second opinion and a third opinion try and find who's the best dentist in the country at this call five references call the cat that'd be the same thing to do but if you think anybody's doing it you're in dreamland they're not buying expertise they can't they can't figure that out either ahead of time no way to judge it they're not actually buying services nor do you want to sell services and they're not buying even results that's not what they're paying for because for the most part in advance they can't predict them they don't believe what you're promising them at best they're hoping and in many cases keeping them won't have much to do with results either what they are really buying what is driving the decision to get into this relationship with you is your confidence your certainty and the control you exercise over them from the beginning so that when you finally prescribe okay here's what you should have and here's the fee for it there are no questions that's really what should happen what should happen is what should not happen as well can i take this out and this out and then how much would it be that shouldn't happen it should be you prescribed it here's the fee and they say yes that's what should happen and that has everything to do with confidence certainty and control and it has almost nothing to do with everything else which brain surgeon do you want the guy who says to you well chances of success here very hard to predict i don't claim to be the best brain surgeon in the country um i work real hard at it i study real hard i kind of like the look of your x-rays and i'll get you the best outcome i possibly can or you want the guy who says you came to the right place you could have gone to 500 hospitals you could have you're in the you're you're in the best hands possible relax we're gonna drill your head open and have you as good as new by nine o'clock tomorrow morning you'll you'll play the piano again any young man i but i never played to be you'll play the piano all right which brain surgeon do you want now logically here's what most people will tell you well that well i want the guy who will tell me the truth but that ain't who they buy from no they want the guy who is certain who is certain there's an entire industry in this country that sells sports picks so you pay money and they tell you who to bet on they can't get you on the phone and analyze the upcoming game well this could happen that could happen if it snows that gives a four-point advantage to these guys and what's all said and done who's gonna win the game i don't know no they gotta say virginia tech's gonna win a game and they're gonna win by four and a half points uh include a field goal in the last two minutes give me five thousand dollars now by the way the good news is the retention of that client has very little to do with whether virginia tech wins the game or wins the game by four and a half points or wins it with a field goal in the last quarter the first quarter it has very little to do with that you would think it has a lot to do with that but it doesn't have much to do with that but it starts with they want certainty here's some coaches and consultants then self-sabotage create self-sabotage number one excessive concern or actually any concern over your credentials or your educational background so there's a whole bunch of people in our field all hung up because they don't have an MBA or they didn't go to college or they didn't go to high school or they barely got out of high school or you know it took them four times to pass the GED or whatever that may be an effort nobody cares except you nobody and nobody's going to ask you okay as long as you like look like you got out and you act like you got out and you talk like you got out nobody cares a lot of people are all concerned about it okay over your experience or your lack thereof because coaching and consulting is more about asking questions and giving answers and it is far more about common sense than it is about anything else you got all the experience you need because you got common sense and because all businesses are the same by the way and they are not different you don't have to know squat about the particular business that you are helping in order to be of enormous help because most of the help you will be will be by asking questions not by giving answers and by dispensing common sense very simple observation all right so I don't know what happened and it's not even relevant what's happened but through a series of events really before we're ready um um Sydney Biddlebarrow's son developing a new consulting and coaching business with and Sydney raise your hands so everybody knows where we are there you go so the guy in New York that owns more restaurants in New York than anybody else really really successful restaurateur um has a property a big themed restaurant that he's put a lot of money in and it's got problems and so possible Sydney's gonna consult with him about fixing these problems now to the best of my knowledge Sydney's never run a restaurant she's never owned a restaurant and probably what she knows specifically about restaurants is how to order wine but she goes and looks and I'm listening on the phone as she's telling me what she's seen and it's a long list of things that common sense tells you are wrong are incongruent with the objectives that he stated for this restaurant and it's a perfectly good list of things that need to be changed didn't require owning a restaurant didn't require going to the restaurant management institute didn't require a it just requires walking in with an open mind an objective attitude about the place some common sense watching people and how they're reacting and saying why in the hell would they be doing that okay doesn't require much more than that then it will require asking a bunch of questions so that the guy kind of feels like he's figuring it out for himself and then everybody's gonna be happy okay so it is not about lack of experience you should worry if you have no common sense that would be a legitimate problem you know but lack of experience no let's see every field I've in a 90 day period I became the most in-demand consultant in the television infomercial industry never produced an infomercial misspelled infomercial couldn't run it couldn't actually do anything physically like run a camera or anything if I touch it it breaks no previous tv experience no previous movie industry experience not about that it's about common sense all right well let's see if there's no call if there's no offer that's probably not good because people are not going to run to their phones right I mean common sense but people put tv shows together all the time we no offer concerns over your financial status versus the clients we're going to talk about that quite a bit well all my clients make more money make more money than I do how dare I tell them we're going to talk about that in a second personal hangups about money we're going to talk about that invisible displays of weakness we can't talk about that so let's jump to 20 experience not required where am I lost my damn here we go okay this is from one of the books you should read if you have it it's called I'm selling the invisible a good book about selling intangibles here's the big point in most professional services you are really not selling expertise because your expertise is assumed and because your prospect can't evaluate your expertise anyway so it's assumed if you make it assumed and he has no way to evaluate your expertise anyway until you actually deliver and because most of the delivery does not revolve around expertise it revolves around asking questions and it revolves around common sense he'll be fine with the amount of expertise that that you have here's my platinum list worry fact largest coaching business in the restaurant industry large information largest information marketing business in the restaurant industry has exactly the same qualifications Sid Sidney does I never owned a restaurant never ran a restaurant never managed a restaurant never built a successful restaurant I don't even think he ever worked in a restaurant and I don't think he knows how to order the right wine so ever been written about in the wall street journal not only doesn't matter nobody asks remarkable Ron I pack auto repair industry it's like Rory to the best of my knowledge Ron has never owned an auto repair shop never managed an auto repair shop never run an auto repair shop I don't know if he can repair a car or not but that's kind of irrelevant I believe when he started he was in the windshield repair business right number one guru to the auto repair industry at O'Keeffe coaching programs in dentistry right a whole bunch of dentist paying at different levels 10,000 a year 30,000 a year 50,000 a year adding a dentist never owned a practice never ran a practice never marketed a practice never drilled a tooth presumably the only expertise he has in dentistry is getting a tooth drilled by a dentist like me with chiropractic in dentistry I can't do either so there's abundant evidence that it ain't the expertise okay how dare you consult and coach people who are more successful than you are well there's a whole bunch of reasons why because the value that you deliver has no relationship to and is not governed by the ratio of financial success between you and the client so for example the thing most people think about is the one all the way at the bottom specialized knowledge and it is legit so I make a lot more money than my doctor I make a lot more money than my CPA I make a lot more money than the guy who's able to fix everything in my house but that does not mean I am not delighted to give him everything he asks for and little does he know considerably more because he can fix the washing machine I don't know how to fix the washing machine he will run over and light the pilot light when the wind blows it out I don't know how to light the pilot light I don't want to know how to light the pilot light I have no passing interest in knowing how to light the pilot light as long as he will run over and light the pilot light he has a piece of knowledge I do not have that makes him enormously valuable to me even though he probably makes about thirty thousand dollars a year say it doesn't matter that's not what it's about all these other values have nothing to do with how much you make versus how much they make here are all the values one fresh eyes just because you walk in and see it for the first time and by that I don't mean necessarily physically walking in I mean that is part of it so he goes to the restaurant she sees it for the first time she's got fresh eyes he's in it all the time okay he's now not seeing all sorts of things he should be seeing bad things good things he don't see him any one of us can walk in and see him right but it doesn't have to be a physical place it could be their ad it could be their brochure it could be their financial statement it could be their management policies whatever area in which you work you have fresh eyes they don't so no matter how smart they are no matter how successful they are they become blind even though they can see and we can walk in and we can see why on earth are you doing it this way why wouldn't you do it this way dumb questions there's enormous value in asking stupid questions I mean just dumb simple questions to to to force them to think about what they are doing I like to have in coaching groups I like to have one dumb one I like to have one that's like behind everybody else okay because he'll ask questions everybody else forgot and it forces everybody to think about the basics again forced thought just slowing them down long enough to get them to think that's a value we provide okay consulting day a lot of the value clients get out of a consulting day with me is their preparation the smart ones some of them say I could have not come being forced to do all this preparation was enough I already got 26 things I found I need to do I don't need 26 more from from you can we just go to lunch you know I mean all right forced thought one of the things about coaching okay is just forcing them to get out of their business for four hours or eight hours or two days turn off the cell phone not be interrupted and bothered all the time and just think they won't do it on their own all right I mean you can teach them to do that but they won't do it because you and I won't do it we all know we should take four hours you know or like those old deal lock yourself in a room for an hour every morning with no resources no nothing and just think about how you could be of greater value and service interest to your customers well it's a great idea how many people are going to do it now buddy somebody's got to go lock them in the room hand them a pad and a pen and tell them you can't come out until you've written down 10 ideas about how to be of greater value and service and interest to your customers and if I open a door in an hour and you don't have 10 ideas on a pad I'm slamming a door and locking it for another hour and eventually you're going to get hungry well see that's a coaching value that requires no expertise right it requires a pad a pad and a key right that's all it requires but it's of enormous value make them defend their ideas more successful people are the less people challenging so pretty soon inevitably they have a whole bunch of people sitting around who agree with everything they think of no matter how dumb or useless or idiotic it may be just making them defend their ideas because when people around or when everybody around you reinforces your own premises and ideas that's like how you wind up in Iraq with no plan on what to do with it after you got it because you sat in a room with a whole bunch of people who reinforced your original premise and didn't force you to defend it that's how you wind up there so just forcing them it's okay if they defend it and they're right or they win the argument or they refuse but at least you made them think about it and argue it with somebody unearthed ignored opportunities that's sort of like fresh eyes very very often you are going to be talking to somebody and they're going to tell you about the thing that's really good that like leaps out at you okay again it doesn't require enormous expertise to because they'll tell it to you but they're ignoring it for one reason or another they either don't like it or they think it's commonplace and routine or whatever but mean there's a lot of clients getting things done in their business because this is what Dan said to do that they would not get done if they couldn't say this is what Dan said to do so my value to carbon copies I don't want them to do anything I just want to be able to put carbon copy a big law firm at the bottom of the letter that's value to me I'm happy to give them a retainer for it and then get them to stay out of my way so for a lot of people value is being able to say well that's what Dan said to do well all right we'll do it if it was his idea it'd never happen well that's value doesn't require a validate their own premises and ideas as being right that's a great value you can deliver if they happen to be right you encourage them you know to go forward sometimes all they need is a little encouragement motivation accountability we talked about recognition this is a big value that we provide it's easy for you to provide see everybody the more successful they are the more likely it is that they are giving themselves their own gold stars and nobody else is giving them good gold stars so the real estate agent in Tupelo who manages to stand up and give a speech everybody tells him how fabulous he was trump goes and gives a speech nobody says anything to him afterwards because he's supposed to be fabulous but he desperately wants somebody to tell him he was fabulous and at some point Tommy probably paid some speech coach $50,000 to watch him and tell him he was fabulous Tyson one of the reasons he has no money is he used to have four full-time people who walked around behind him and their job was to say you're the man Mike all right now that's sort of an extreme version of this but but there's a lot of people looking for somebody to stand around and say you're the man Mike we provide them an environment where they get recognition if it's in a coaching environment the group gives them recognition if it's in a consulting environment they get recognition my one-on-one calls so part of my platinum program I had 34 people they all get 20 minutes on the phone with me privately eight months eight times during the year by the way I do them all in one day and the last few people better be good but I'd say 25% of those calls are recognition calls 25% of recognition calls meaning they they're doing well so they're calling to tell me how well they're doing and what smart thing they did and what success they just had because who else they go to tell and we got anybody else to tell so they tell me now I say terrific genius brilliant it's like the beer commercial you have beer commercial with them two little talking guys what beer is that you know and the one says we're going to put them in a case and the guy says brilliant all right that's what I'm doing I'm sitting there saying brilliant send me a copy of that I got to see it got you off the phone feels great who else was going to do that for nobody all right one good to get it at home one good to get it at the office one good to get it anywhere all right then some of them are accountability calls they want somebody to ask them did you do the thing you said you were going to do last month no why didn't you do it you got a little right so some of them are accountability calls hardly any of them are heavy lifting calls oh I got this terrible problem what are the 412 ways I should fix it I don't have those calls a lot of it is this connections putting people together we're talking about that a little later so what's important about this list is this is all value there's only one thing on the whole list that is actually related to expertise experience specialized knowledge knowing how to do one thing well that they don't know how to do you only need one thing by the way that's all you need and everybody in here knows how to do something pretty well the rest of the values have nothing to do with that right and these are the values that will keep people right they may buy because the last one and a perception of it but these are what will keep them in the game yes you talked about the how unnecessary it is really to have credentials however when you do have the credentials when do you actually include them or when would you be really sabotaging you by putting your stuff out there well first of all you misquote me all right okay and it's an important distinction I have not once today and probably never but certainly not today said it is important to have credentials I have actually said quite the opposite from here it can be useful to have them but I have never said it is important to have them I said what is important to have is a carefully constructed reputation there is a world of difference between the two we're not just playing semantics and it's really important not to be in that trap of credentials right but it is important to construct a reputation and then all the evidence you have that supports the reputation put it out there you can't overdo it so if you've got 420 articles that have been published somewhere send 420 articles don't send 42 right if you got 500 testimonials send all 500 of them don't send 50 you might want to highlight 50 you might want it so you put it all out there but it is more about your constructed reputation than it is did you go to Harvard not go to Harvard did you get an MBA not get an MBA are you a member of this that or the other thing or not a member of this that you know or the other thing those are credentials so if you have those though my coincidence before this you know do you include them or sure yeah they're no harm no foul okay at at worst and and at best they may be reassuring to somebody because I noticed that there's car practice for example who are coaches who let's jump over to page 25 third big big thing of the day is success in the field requires a willingness to play the game meaning if you are absolutely determined to let people make buying and retention decisions on their own through logic based on the core values you deliver you are not going to make a lot of money you're going to have a lot of trouble keeping clients and you're probably not going to as much fun as you could have otherwise if you view it as as a game you are willing to play and you view it all as a performance first of all you think of it that way you'll think about it better um then here's the criteria for success one is the performance has to delight the audience now again that's all about their values not yours see so we so let's take coaching you're gonna if you we put 10 billionaires in the group you're going to have slow students and you're going to have champions right so no matter what you do billionaires non-billionaires you're going to have slow students and you're going to have champions you are going to have somebody in coaching a coaching group or even in a consulting relationship who it seems to you didn't go in anywhere like at the end of the year they're in the same place they were before okay but see that's your value trying to be imposed on them that they should have gotten somewhere that's not up to you it isn't up to you to define it it isn't up to you to impose it it isn't it isn't up to you to judge them by it it's only important that they are delighted that's all that is important okay so whatever values they got if they're happy that's all that is important and different people are delighted by different things I had somebody in coaching group for three years you want to reason this person was there was to show all that is that three years I've never asked a question start showing stuff on the next question you know it saves time for somebody else I did the work last I would have to think it's hard he's delighted fine that's what it's about I wouldn't be delighted it's just like I just said on the break if I made 10,000 bucks a week in the seminar I'd be running back tapping my watch hey Kennedy let's go let's rock and roll here okay but if you guys want to stroll back who am I to impose my value system on you I can skip some pages save save save voice no matter to me it's okay by me right and it's not up to me to determine what's going to delight you other than to make sure I try and delight everybody so if you view it as performance then its obligation is to delight the audience and enrich you that's its two obligations right so everything else is great and people will get what they're going to get so are you willing to play the game are you willing to manipulate people are you willing to take control of people are you willing to only entertain those who wish only to be entertained you know in other words are you willing to crawl inside there and find out what it is that really is going to make him delighted and figure out a way to get it done and do it in a way that extracts the most amount of money from him doesn't do any harm and hopefully doesn't good that's a big asset this is from actually this is based on it's pulled from a book called thick-faced black heart which if you haven't read it you should it is written by a Asian female business consultant and this is a very good thing she identified is that in our culture the idea of deception is viewed very differently than in other cultures in other cultures it's a given that deception is a normal and routine and ordinary and expected and necessary part of any game here we view it in a spiritual emotional context that deception is evil so we are unwilling to admit that we are engaged in it and therefore we're handicapped in doing it really well and so most people will immediately protest that they do not I don't deceive people I don't engage in deception and so the last female to tell me that about business had implants and was wearing a push-up bra to show them off is that deception or is that not deception I've always said you know conversation with Lee years ago you know she was telling me about the guy she was dating and what she was going through I said you better stop that because eventually he's going to find out like who you really are and I mean and I mean you know well that's going to be a problem you know like I'm going to cook for him you're going to do what what you know what have you cooked three times in the last decade I mean and twice yeah I mean it's just but we all do it don't we we all put ourselves on our best behavior at certain times but our best behavior is not our normal and continuous and constant behavior so isn't that deception the answer to the question does this make me look fat who who in here wishes to give a completely truthful frank and unvarnished answer see so it's not a question of do we engage in it it's really a question of degrees and competency at it but in a sense all advertising all sales all marketing all promotion has some degree of some element of deception in it so deception manipulation psychological control of people it's what we do now it's just a question of are you going to do it really really well or not early on I this is from a novel I was sort of life-shaping stuff because it was very early there's a fictional character um badly portrayed in a tv series that wasn't awful but it wasn't good on a and a called called our dear our near old wolf and near old wolf is a detective who is very fat and therefore doesn't leave his house if he can help it so he solves mysteries without going anywhere and he makes everybody come to him and and so every novel and there's I don't know 80 of them before Rex died did they've written some sense with which is deception in and of itself by the way a massive amount of fiction now is turned out by dead guys the estate hires journeymen writers puts them in a room and they publish the stuff under the name of the dead guy Robert Lutland now is these books out he's been dead for six years there's near a wolf novels out rex doubts been dead for 25 years now find prints somewhere it's disclosed but if you're not really careful you think you're buying a book written by rex doubt and you're buying the next installment in the near a wolf series which you are but Rex has been dead for a long time anyway so near a wolf is this rather fat detective who likes gourmet food so he has a cook and he won't leave the house and so everybody's got to come to him and he pups us around every morning in with his orchids on his roof and his greenhouse so he absolutely won't see anybody before 11 o'clock and the place could be on fire and he won't come down in his elevator until precisely at 11 o'clock and the world could be sitting there waiting for him he ain't coming down until 11 o'clock he doesn't like to work so he only works when people like forcing to work and they like have to throw money at him and cajole him and beg him and push him and prod him and nudge him in order to get him to work and uh so on one of your pages on the body with us but it's page 27 there's a block out of a near a wolf book so i read this stuff like in my teens and i'm thinking this guy's got it right right this this this is exactly the way it ought to be right totally contrary of course to my experience at the time but i'm thinking this guy's got this is like what you should get to is and see it always it's amusing to me that like the president of the United States treks over to Bosnia and wherever the hell he goes i never have understood it ever i'm president of the United States you're king of whatever or president of some pissant country and you want to talk to me you're coming to the white house i ain't leaving i'm getting in there four years from now you can pry me out in between i ain't going anywhere i'm certainly not flying into some dangerous place where people want to shoot me protest or why should i do that i'm president of the United States and we give money to everybody so the other reason you're coming to see me is you want money why should i come see you right i i i never never have understood it i mean honest to god i wouldn't leave for anything i mean go over to congress to give a speech screw that but i'm all come over here and stand on the law and i'll give a speech i ain't going over there i never did i've never gotten that you know so i i read this there are wolf stuff and you it's the same of course book after book after book after book after what and so people are like putting a hundred thousand dollar checks on the desk and by the way these books were written in the 50s uh putting a hundred thousand dollar checks on the desk and he's got to think about it overnight as to whether or not he's going to take it you know and they're all coming to him and i'm thinking that guy's got it right and sounds like implanted in the you know in the brain there's a non-fiction guy very famous detective there's an autobiography of him it's great book uh and the guy's name and it's his real name it's of course so like rory fat and his name being in a restaurant industry could you be better right but i mean be really bad if you were like president of neutrosystems but uh but but as a guy in a restaurant field that's good right um so this guy's name is jj arms or arm mess if you pronounce it correctly and what's cool is he has no arms so he has artificial arms with hooks and he's a private detective and he's really famous he's done some big cases marlon brandos kidnapping was a case of his and so forth and his autobiography is worth reading but the best thing about reading it is and i don't know if he's still in business because this is 10 years ago but if you're going to hire him the conversation has to start with a hundred thousand dollars on the desk and if you don't bring a hundred thousand dollars there's no point coming that's how the conversation starts i'm thinking that's a good way for conversation to start that's like a good rule right i mean and that's really where my all relationships began with a paid day of consulting came from right now i didn't start with the hundred thousand but that's where i got the concept from i said okay so why should there be a free lunch why should there be a free meeting why should there be a free anything why don't everything start with somebody putting a check on a desk let's start there rather than where most people start and so you're way ahead of where most people start if you just start there let's talk about managing expectations next thing we'll jump over to 28 i'm going to show you this is again benton bartender son osborne did the ad so therefore they must be good i'm going to show you for those of you who haven't seen it and for those who have seen it it's fine anyway but let's take time tonight to show you the episode of the pentatella bullshit show about bottled water and it is a it is a lesson in the power of managing expectations and what people then will accept is true and and or acceptable to them based on expectations and the example i've always used in marketing seminars is southwest airlines because south southwest airlines of course delivers the least it's it's a god awful experience from beginning to end if you aren't there two hours before flight time you know you're going to have the orange pass and you're going to be in the middle seat between two fat people eating buckets of chicken that's how you're going to fly across if you're flying across country there it's a bus southwest is oh yeah oh yeah cleveland of vegas by way of memphis st lewis san francisco and tuscaloosa right it's our greyhound bus with wings right and pretty much half the people flying it should be on the greyhound bus would have been on a greyhound bus if the southwest ticket wasn't so cheap you now amenities are being taken away from the big airlines but you know for years i mean you got no amenities you got no napa you got a crappy little seat and the flight attendant sings this is their deal right year after year after year they have the highest customer satisfaction ratings and they deliver the least if american airlines treated you like southwest treats you you'd be burning down the terminal southwest doesn't you're happy why because they manage your expectation to start with okay they're very they make no pretentious they're very clear about what they are in the early years before anybody knew what they were and they had to condition everybody that this was okay that you're going to come we're not going to sign a seat you're going to stand in line we're going to give you a plastic pass we're going to call you by groups you all stampede down the thing is a herd elbow each other out of the way as best you can get whatever seat you can fight for we're going to give you a back even if you're flying across the country you're going to be on play for seven hours you're getting a bag of peanuts one coat that's it and we're going to get you there what when when when they first figured that out you know this was like a new concept so how did they sell it they were very clear they were very clear about what we are and what we aren't but we're going to take off on time and we're going to get you there on time and by god they do keep that promise 18 people still standing up trying to put their shit away they're rolling down a runway okay no other airline doesn't every other airline farter on try to get martha's stuff put away they're moving martha's on crutches try to put they don't care we're going okay best thing about them right they keep they got a promise they keep the promise it's pretty simple they manage expectations all right so the lesson of southwest is people will tolerate anything if their expectations are properly managed about what it is they are going to tolerate that's lesson number one about managing expectations now reputation expectations how it turns ordinary access to so trump has a reputation for not wanting to shake hands it's very well known and so you know shake hands with donald trump you don't like to shake hands so we had donald trump speak at a jeff caller event and arranged for a little private session with a few of the speakers and me and jeff and ron le gran and trump walks in and voluntarily shake hands with everybody right everybody's like blown away they're still talking about it two years ago trump shook my hand incredible well kind of an ordinary act don't you think i mean nobody's mesmerized when you're i shake their hand but because he set it up in advance everybody's mesmerized so he's in and out there in 10 minutes taking pictures like you know heading for the bus uh illegible autograph on ship everybody's happy thrilled positive word of mouth because he shook their hands all right because he set it up in advance that he don't shake hands expectations manage well to turn an ordinary act into an extraordinary act the importance of the introduction any speaker will tell you that the results on the stage have an enormous amount to do with who introduces them and what that person says or doesn't say all right i can tell you for example all the years of the peter lovance here's here's an absolute known for me if peter mc the event and introduced me versus his wife mc the event and introducing me a 10 swing in my dollars per head in sales no other variable okay same thing said made a difference who was saying it any speaker knows it well you have an introduction one way or another to everything you do all right so almost never do you start a seminar the way i started this seminar of walking up and starting the seminar i don't care if they've been to 500 year seminars somebody should introduce you and if you're there to get money somebody should introduce the introducer who introduces you and one of them should be given testimony introduction matters and those people can manage the expectations of what's going to happen better than you can so there's a whole science to how happy people are with what they get how controllable they are and what they assume about what they are getting based on what they are told
